tokens: For clarity, a version of this audio file is included that has some noise reduction applied.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 85 hours, 21 minutes into the flight.
A little more than 20 minutes since we've been in touch with Apollo 8.
We should reacquire in about 18 minutes.
To recap a bit, the crew was given a few hours extra rest, particularly Jim Lovell.
On the 7th rev, Bill Anders, presumably, is also getting some nap time prior to the trans-Earth burn a little later tonight.
One or two things might be pointed up from today's revolutions around the Earth [means Moon], we've noted the temperature excursions that have occurred.
They weren't entirely unpredicted but the variance interested people here on the consoles.
Excursions over a 50 degree range.
Another point that's proving interesting here, with the passage of each rev, is the fact that our apogee tends to grow ever so slightly and our perigee tends to shrink.
This has not been the experience in Earth orbital flight.

The apogee tends to shrink ever so slightly and the perigee usually remains stable coming down somewhat but it - the Earth orbital experience is explained by the ever so slight amount of drag exercised on spacecraft at perigee which tends to pull down the apogee to slow the spacecraft somewhat at perigee and the effect of it comes in at the high point of the orbit and tends to drop it down somewhat.
Somewhat the opposite effect seems to be taking place in these revolutions around the Moon.
We have plots here on the first rev of 60.5 [nautical] miles [112.0 km] for apogee versus 60.9 [112.8 km] perigee.
The next rev 60.4 apogee versus 61.7 perigee, then a 62 versus 60.1, a 62.3 [115.4 km] apogee versus a 59.8 [110.7 km] and the curve continues in that way.
It's slight but it is interesting and it does not conform to the Earth orbital experience.
Again another reason for wanting to fly this navigational-operational Apollo 8 mission.
At 85 hours, 24 minutes into the flight; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The onboard audio clip that follows covers the period up to and including the historic TV broadcast that is to come.
It therefore gives an interesting "behind the scenes" point of view as to how the telecast played out within the spacecraft.
A fuller description of the Apollo 8 Christmas Eve broadcast is provided at 85:44:50
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Again, we have included a version of the above audio with noise reduction applied.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston here; 85 hours, 39 minutes and we're very nearly at the acquisition point.
Only 10 seconds away.
And we should, if we're on plan, move right into a television transmission.
The time of 85 hours, 40 minutes has been passed to the crew.
The prime site for this picture will be the Goldstone Station from California.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're getting telemetry now via Honeysuckle Creek the dish in Australia.
And no word yet on Goldstone.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Getting a carrier noise now.
It's probably indicative of transmission coming.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: That is, lockup of the High Gain Antenna gimbals, not those on the IMU.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: There's still no calls, although we're a minute and a half - we're a minute and a half into acquisition.
The capsule communicator has been advised to pass to the crew, when we acquire, that all of the systems look good.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Two minutes now since we did acquire the spacecraft.
Rather noisy data.
The data on the 9th revolution around the Moon, with an apogee of 63 [nautical] miles [116.7 km] of a perigee of 58.9 [nautical] miles [109.1 km].
Velocity, 5,352 feet per second.
We've got a picture here, and we've got a voice to go with it.
Bill Anders.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The following audio clip begins as Apollo 8 emerges from behind the Moon to begin its ninth revolution.
The historic TV broadcast commences immediately upon Acquisition of Signal from the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Loud and clear and initial look at your systems are good.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Houston, Apollo 8.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: This video of the TV broadcast is supplied courtesy of Mark Gray.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: H.264 MP4 video file.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We've got a picture, Apollo 8.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
We've got the T - Roger.
We've got the TV [garble].
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: How does the picture look, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Loud and clear.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The TV look okay?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: That's very good.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Welcome from the Moon, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Houston, you're seeing a view of the Earth taken below the lunar horizon.
We're going to follow the track until the terminator, where we will turn the spacecraft and give you a view of the long shadowed terrain at the terminator, which should come in quite well in the TV.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We're theorizing here that that bright spot in the top left center of your picture is the Earth.
It's not very clear.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We don't know whether you can see it from the TV screen, but the Moon is nothing but a milky white.
Completely void.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We're changing the cameras to the other window now.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: For the next 23 minutes, Frank Borman directs a TV broadcast from the spacecraft that he has been concocting in his head for a while now.
Realising the historic significance of the flight, and given his own faith, he has arranged a TV show that will extend across the lighted part of the Moon's near side.
He has worked out that the deep shadows of the terminator at the end of the show will look particularly good on the crude TV system they have.

The climax of his orchestration, however, will be to have the crew read passages from the Book of Genesis that relate to the creation of the Universe just as they pass into night, and then to keep quiet; to allow time for the significance of the moment to sink into the audience.
In the view of this author, whose professional life is in broadcasting, Borman's vision is masterly in its execution.
The power of the broadcast has never been forgotten, even if it has been eclipsed by Neil Armstrong's first step.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The ability of the TV system to reproduce the subtle tones of the lunar landscape lit by a high Sun is limited and until they near the terminator, the definition is poor.

Journal contributors, René and Jonathan Cantin, have produced anextraordinary video file, viewable on YouTubethat covers the entire transmission and which uses a split-screen to illustrate and annotate the features in the landscape they are flying over.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Download theApollo 8 TV Broadcast 4 file.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo 8, coming to you live from the Moon.
We've had to switch the TV camera now.
We showed you first a view of Earth as we've been watching it for the past 16 hours.
Now we're switching so that we can show you the Moon that we've been flying over at 60 miles altitude for the last 16 hours.
Bill Anders, Jim Lovell, and myself have spent the - the day before Christmas up here doing experiments, taking pictures, and firing our spacecraft engines to maneuver around.
What we'll do now is follow the trail that we've been following all day and take you on through to a lunar sunset.
The Moon is a different thing to each one of us.
I think that each one of - each one carries his own impression of what he's seen today.
I know my own impression is that it's a vast, lonely, forbidding-type existence, or expanse of nothing, that looks rather like clouds and clouds of pumice stone...
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: ...inviting place to live or work.
Jim, what have you thought most about?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Well, Frank, my thoughts are very similar.
The vast loneliness up here of the Moon is awe inspiring, and it makes you realize just what you have back there on Earth.
The Earth from here is a grand oasis in the big vastness of space.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Bill, what do you think?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: I think the thing that impressed me the most were the lunar sunrises and sunsets.

These in particular bring out the stark nature of the terrain, and the long shadows really bring out the relief that is here and hard to see in this very bright surface that we're going over right now.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: You're describe - that's not color, Bill.
Describe some of the physical features of what you're showing the people.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Apollo 8, Houston.
We're not receiving a picture now.
Over.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We are now coming on to Smyth's Sea, a small mare region covered with a dark, level material.
There is a fresh, bright, impact crater on the edge towards us and a mountain range on the other side.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: These mountains are the Pyrenees.
[onboard] Is that right?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Apollo 8, we are not receiving modulation on the signal; we have - do have sync.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Are you reading us?
Apollo 8, Houston.
[onboard] Maybe it's turned off, Bill.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Apollo 8, we're reading you loud and clear, but no picture.
We have no modulation.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
We understand.
Take a look now.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: How about now?
Apollo.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Loud and clear.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Good picture.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: What you're seeing as we cross Smyth's Sea are the craters Castner and Gilbert...
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: ...and what we've noticed especially, that you cannot see from the Earth, are these small bright impact craters that dominate the lunar surface.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The horizon here is very, very stark.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: The sky is pitch black, and the Earth - or the Moon, rather, excuse me, is quite light.
And the contrast between the sky and the Moon is a vivid dark line.

Coming into view of the camera now are some interesting old double ring craters, some interesting features that are quite common in the mare region and have been filled by some material - the same consistency of the maria and the same color.
Here are three or four of these interesting features.
Further on the horizon you see the Pyr - [Pause].
[onboard] These are the Pyrenees, aren't they?...
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Okay.
The mountains coming up now are heavily impacted with numerous craters whose central peaks you can see in many of the larger ones.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Actually, I think the best way to describe this area is a vastness of black and white, absolutely no color.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The sky up here is also rather forbidding, foreboding expanse of blackness, with no stars visible when we're flying over the Ear - over the Moon in daylight.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: You can see by the numerous craters...
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: ...that this planet has been bombarded through the eons with numerous small asteroids and meteoroids, pock marking the surface every square inch.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: And one of the amazing features of the surface is the roundness that most of the craters - seems that most of them have a round mound type of appearance instead of sharp, jagged rocks.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Only the very newest features have any sharp definition to them, and eventually they get eroded down by the constant bombardment of small meteorites.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: How's the picture now, Houston?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Houston, are you reading us?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Our picture is - Loud and clear, and the picture looks real fine.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Can you see the two large craters to the - just to the right of our track, Houston?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: That's affirmative.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The very bright features you see are the new impact craters, and the longer a crater has been on the surface of the Moon, why, the more mottled and subdued it becomes.
Some of the...
[Stops himself.
Long pause].
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: The Flight Director thinks voice contact has been lost and asks Mattingly to check with them.
In fact, Bill was cut off by Frank who worries that Bill is getting a bit too esoteric with his terminology.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 8, we've apparently lost your voice; the picture is still good.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Houston, we're passing over an area that's just to the east of Smyth's Sea now, in checking our charts.
Smyth's Sea is coming up in a few minutes.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Apollo 8, if you go to P00 and Accept, why, we'll uplink some information.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: This is so Mission Control can uplink a revised state vector into the slots in memory normally reserved for the Lunar Module.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We are now coming up towards the terminator, and I hope that soon we'll be able to show you the varying contrast of white as we go into the darkness.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Houston, we're in P00, and you have the computer.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We're now approaching a series of small impact craters.
There is a dark area between us and them which could possibily be an old lava flow.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: You can see the large mountains on the horizon now ahead of the spacecraft, to the north of our track.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: We estimate it's about 325 miles to the horizon just to help your reference.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The intensity of the Sun's reflection in this area...
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: ...makes it difficult for us to distinguish the features we see on the surface, and I suppose it's even harder on the television, but as we approach the terminator and the shadows become longer, you'll see a marked change.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: There's a very dark crater in the filling material in this valley in front of us now.
It's rather unusual in that it's sharply defined, yet it's dark all over its interior walls, whereas most new-looking craters are very bright interior.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Small impact crater in front of us now in the little mare.
Well defined, quite new, and another one approaching.
The spacecraft is facing north.
From our track, we are going sideways to our left.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: You're now seeing the Sea of Crises coming over the horizon.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: We believe the crater, the large dark crater between the spacecraft and the Sea of Crises is Condorcet crater.
The Sea of Crises is amazingly smooth as far as the horizon, past this rather rough, mountainous region in front of the spacecraft.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Apollo 8, we're through with the computer, if you go back to Block, and it looks like we are getting a lot of reflection off your window now.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
We'll switch windows.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: How's that look now, Ken?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: That's real fine.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Apollo 8, can you tell us which window you're looking out?
And there's a large crater, looks like it's sticking up in the upper right hand corner of our picture.
Can you identify that one?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
We're just about to lose our lock here.
That's why we're slowing up a little bit.
[onboard] You're alright.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: We see the Sea of Crises in front of us now.
We are looking out the left hand rendezvous window.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: We...
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Houston, how you reading us now?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The crater you see on the horizon is the Sea of Crises.
[Pause through static.] How are you reading us, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Loud and clear, Apollo 8, and we have a picture that's good.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
We're getting a lot of static.
The Sea of Crises is in front of us on the horizon, and the dark crater Picard can be seen in the middle.
We're now breaking the Moon's sunrise or the spacecraft's sunset.
This is an area that the Sun has just recently come up on the Moon.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: See the mare we're over now has a mottled look about it,...
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: ...but not very heavily cratered, so it must be relatively new.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: This is the Sea of Fertility, and we're coming up on a large crater, the delta rim variety.
[Pause.] Has a strange circular cracks pattern around the middle of it.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: The crater that you're seeing now is about 30 or 40 miles across.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: How's your picture quality, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: This is phenomenal!
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: There's an interesting rille directly in front of the spacecraft now, running along the edge of a small mountain.
Rather sinuous shape with right angle turns.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: This area just to the west of the Sea of Crises is called the Marsh of Sleep, and to the west of that is the Sea of Tranquillity.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Can you see the fracture patterns going across the mare in front of us now, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: That doesn't quite stand out.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
The series of cracks or faults across the middle of the mare; they drop down in about three steps to the south.
The parallel fault pattern to the north has a drop-down in the center.
[Long pause.] [onboard] I think you may be going too far now, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: I hope that all of you back on Earth can see what we mean when we say that it's a rather foreboding horizon, a very - rather...
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: ...stark and unappetizing looking place.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: We're now going over our...
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: ...approaching one of our future landing sites...
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: ...selected in this smooth region to...
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: ...called the Sea of Tranquility - smooth in order to make it easy for the initial landing attempts in order to preclude having to dodge mountains.
Now you can see the long shadows of the lunar sunrise.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: In this instance, the terms 'sunset' and 'sunrise' are both applicable.
In the case of the spacecraft and its path around the Moon, it is about to go into the Moon's shadow, so for Apollo 8 and its crew, the Sun is about to set.
At the same time, the landscape they are passing over is experiencing a lunar morning and they will soon cross the Moon's sunrise terminator.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We are now approaching lunar sunrise, and for all the people back on Earth, the crew of Apollo 8 has a message that we would like to send to you.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: In the beginning, God created the Heaven and the Earth.
And the Earth was without form and void, and darkness was upon the face of the deep.
And the spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters, and God said, "Let there be light." And there was light.
And God saw the light, that it was good, and God divided the light from the darkness.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: And God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night.
And the evening and the morning were the first day.
And God said, "Let there be a firmament in the midst of the waters.
And let it divide the waters from the waters." And God made the firmament and divided the waters which were under the firmament from the waters which were above the firmament.
And it was so.
And God called the firmament Heaven.
And the evening and the morning were the second day.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And God said, "Let the waters under the Heavens be gathered together into one place.
And let the dry land appear." And it was so.
And God called the dry land Earth.
And the gathering together of the waters called he seas.
And God saw that it was good.
And from the crew of Apollo 8, we close with good night, good luck, a Merry Christmas and God bless all of you - all of you on the good Earth.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston.
The speakers in the order that they read from what we believe to be chapters from Genesis were Bill Anders and Jim Lovell, and close out with Frank Borman.
That's both a biblical and a geological lesson that none of us will forget.
At 86 hours, 9 minutes into the flight; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, how do you read?
Apollo 8.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Loud and clear, Apollo 8.
And thank you for a very good show.
We have a maneuver PAD for you when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Good comm was never a given on these missions and Frank may be a little non-plussed that Houston is right back to business without any further words of appreciation.
Fortunately, this is clarified in the exchange that follows.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, Apollo 8.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Apollo 8, read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
Are we off the air now?
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: That's affirmative, Apollo 8.
You are.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Did you read everything that we had to say there?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Loud and clear.
Thank you for a real good show.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay.
Now, Ken, we'd like to get all squared away for TEI here.
Can you give us some good words like you promised?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Yes, sir.
I have a maneuver PAD.
I think we'd like to start by dumping the tape.
If we can have that, I have your...
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: ...TEI-10 maneuver PAD, and then we will run through a systems brief.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I understand this is the maneuver PAD that we will use for TEI.
Is that correct?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: And you got the tape, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Thank you.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Ready to copy, Ken.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Roger.

TEI-10; SPS/G&N; 45597; minus 0.40, plus 1.57; 089:19:15.64; plus 3518.9, minus 0151.3, minus 0034.6; 180, 007, 000; November Alpha, plus 0018.6; 3522.3, 3:18, 3501.9; 42, 092.8, 25.3; boresight star, Scorpi Delta - another name for it is Dschubba - down 07.1, left 4.5; plus 07.48, minus 165.00; 1299.5, 36300, 146:50:05; primary star, Sirius; secondary, Rigel; 129, 155, 010; four quads, 15 second ullage; horizon on the 2.9 window line at T minus 3; use high-speed procedure with minus Mike Alpha.
Over.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:The PAD is a preliminary version for the burn that will return the Apollo 8 crew to Earth at the end of Rev 10.Systems:The burn will be made using the SPS engine, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):45,597 pounds (20,682 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.40° and +1.57°.Time of ignition (Noun 33):89 hours, 19 minutes, 15.64 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +3,518.9 (+1,072.6); Y, -151.3 (-46.1); Z, -34.6 (-10.5).

The large positive number in the X direction implies a large prograde component, essentially adding to their orbital velocity, exactly what would be expected from an escape manoeuvre.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 180°; Pitch, 7°; Yaw, 0°.

The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform which itself has been aligned per theLOI-2 REFSMMAT.Expected apogee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):Not applicable.

Being initiated around the Moon, the apogee of the resulting orbit around Earth is too large to register on the computer.Expected perigee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):18.6 nautical miles (34.4 km).Delta-VT:3,522.3 fps (1,073.6 m/s).
The total sum of the three velocity components.Burn duration or burn time:3 minutes, 18 seconds.Delta-VC:3,501.9 fps.

This figure will be entered into the EMS to allow it to shut down the engine as a backup in case the G&N system fails to do so.Sextant star:Star 42 (Peacock, or Alpha Pavonis) visible in sextant when shaft and trunnion angles are 92.8° and 25.3° respectively.Boresight star:Dschubba, or Delta Scorpii.COAS Pitch Angle:Down 7.1°.COAS X Position Angle:Left 4.5°.The next five parameters all relate to re-entry, during which an important milestone is "Entry Interface," defined as being 400,000 feet (121.92 km) altitude.

In this context, a more important milestone is when atmospheric drag on the spacecraft imparts a deceleration of 0.05 g.Expected splashdown point (Noun 61):7.48° north, 165° west; which is in the mid-Pacific.Range to go at the 0.05 g event:1,299.5 nautical miles.
To set up their EMS (Entry Monitor System) before re-entry, the crew need to know the expected distance the CM would travel from the 0.05 g event to landing.
This figure will be decremented by the EMS based on signals from its own accelerometer.Expected velocity at the 0.05 g event:36,300 fps.
This is another entry for the EMS.

It is entered into the unit's Delta-V counter and will be decremented based on signals from its own accelerometer.Predicted GET of 0.05 g event:146 hours, 50 minutes and 5 seconds GET.GDC Align stars:Stars to be used for GDC Align purposes are Sirius and Rigel.GDC Align angles:Roll, 129°; pitch, 155°; yaw, 10°.There are four additional notes given in the PAD.
An ullage burn of 15 seconds should be made by all four RCS quads to settle the contents of the half-empty SPS tanks prior to the burn.
This is to minimise the chance of helium gas being ingested when the engine ignites.
The Moon's horizon should be lined up on the rendezvous window's 2.9° line at three minutes prior to ignition.
They would use a high-speed procedure with -MA (major axis) in the sums.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.

TEI PAD as follows: SPS/G&N; 45597; minus 0.40, plus 1.57; 089:19:15.64; plus 3518.9, minus 0151.3, minus 0034.6; 180, 007, 000; not applicable, plus 0018.6; 3522.3, 3:18, 3501.9; 42, 092.8, 25.3; Scorpi Delta, Dschubba, down 07.1, left 4.5; plus 07.48, minus 165.00; 1299.5, 36300, 146:50:05; Sirius, Rigel, 129, 155, 010; four quads, 15 seconds; 2.9-degree window line at TIGminus 3; high-speed procedure, minus MA.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: That's correct, Apollo 8.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Ken, this is Frank.
I want to - I want to make one thing certain.
This the load that we're to use to burn with, right?
This is not just a PAD data for a [TEI-]10 abort?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Okay, Apollo 8.
We will update this PAD prior to the burn.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Oh, you will?
Okay.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Yes, sir.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Say again.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 86 hours, 18 minutes into the flight.
Just a word or two on where the crew is looking.
They're particularly - they identified the track extremely well as they moved along.
But some of the areas were not so well identified because of the reading which concluded their pass.
The reading came while they were moving across [the Sea of] Tranquility in a generally westerly direction.
The look angle is to the northwest and to a series of mountains, rimming the northwest edge of the Sea of Tranquility.
Earlier we, you recall, they pointed out the Sea of Crises, concluding that the Picard Craters.
And immediately after we lost the picture lock, we went back to work with this update on - with this tape.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 8.
Houston.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Go ahead, Houston.
Apollo 8.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Roger.
I'm reading you with a lot of background noise.
Can you read me clearly?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Okay.
I'm going to give you a quick summary of the systems.
Basically, all systems are good.
In respect to your return trajectory, we can still get to the mid-Pacific line at 146 hours by waiting as late as the 13th rev.
After 138 seconds of the burn, you're on your way home.
The weather in the recovery area looks good.
Apollo 8, did you call?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Continue, Houston.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Apollo 8.
Houston.
Could we have the High Gain for a little bit longer?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We broke scan on it, Ken.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Oh Roger.
You're coming in loud and clear now.
Did you copy my trajectory information?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We're on omni B though.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Rog.
That's fine.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Say again, please.
Go ahead.
We are 130 - Will you say it again, please?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Wilco.
[Pause.] Apollo 8.
First, if you can spare, we'd like to have the High Gain to complete the dump.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Stand by.
We'll try to get it for you.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: In a couple of minutes there, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Roger.
Thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, Apollo 8.
While we're...
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Apollo 8 - while we are waiting for the High Gain, I will continue the trajectory summary.
We can still get back to the mid-Pacific line in 146 hours from the thirteenth rev, and you're on your way after 138 seconds of the burn.
That's 138 seconds, gets you clear of the butterfly region.
We recommend not trying reignitions or restart after 20 seconds.
If you go beyond 20 seconds, this may get the trajectory beyond the correction - RCS correction capability to a free return.
The weather in recovery area is good.
We have an AOS following TEI of 89 plus 28 plus 39, and an AOS without TEI of 89 plus 37 plus 24.
During the burn, you may notice a slight change in chamber pressure and tank pressures due to the fuel exhaustion in the storage tank and going to the sump tank.
This may occur somewhere around 2 to 5 seconds into the burn.
It'll be a small change in pressures in both systems.
Going down the systems, all systems are Go.
In ECS, we want to stop water boiling after TEI for trajectory purposes.
Your water dump situation looks good; you should be good to greater than 105 hours.
We'll try to hold off the water dump until after MCC-5.
In the EPS, we'd like to stir the cryos prior to TLI - correction TEI.
The next purge on the fuel cells will occur at approximately 92 hours and that will be both hydrogen and oxygen.
Your battery status: battery A, 34.9; battery B, 39.1; and Charlie, 38.5.
We have single tank cryo capability.
SPS: looking at the performance on the previous burns, you can anticipate a normal burn taking approximately 3.7 seconds in excess of the computed values.
Engine performance looks nominal, and all parameters have been steady.
RCS looks good; all four quads according to the computer programs have approximately the same capacity.
You have a good REFSMMAT to take you through TEI.
We'll have a post-TEI PTC attitude for you in a few minutes, and that just about wraps up what we have on the systems.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
Thank you, Houston.
We appreciate the summary.
We're trying to get High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Rog.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I think we have it.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: You do have the High Gain (antenna).
Now, Ken, as I understand it, if it shuts down after 20 seconds of burn, you don't want us to try to relight it.
Is that what you said?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Stand by.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Apollo 8, the intent was; do not delay ignitions beyond 20 seconds.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Oh, do not delay ignition beyond 20 seconds.
Roger.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: That's affirm.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay.
You want me to start it on bank A and then switch to B again, like we did on LOI, right?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: That's affirmative.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Did you put in this PAD for us?
Should P30 and 40 be in our computer now?
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Apollo 8, that's negative.
We had not uplinked this PAD.
We'll put this one in on the next pass.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay.
Roger.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 86 hours, 33 minutes.
A part of the information passed up to the crew during that last series of conversations was the information they will use for their Trans-Earth Injection maneuver.
This is preliminary information.
We do anticipate that it will be updated, probably on the next revolution.

These figures, as they were read up to the crew, are as follows: the time of ignition is 89 hours, 19 minutes and 16 seconds; the burn duration will be 3 minutes, 17.8 seconds; and that will give us a nominal change in velocity of 3,522.3 feet per second, and the maneuver will occur at 174 degrees east longitude and 9 degrees, 17 minutes south latitude over the Moon.
This would give us a nominal return time to Earth of 146 hours, 49 seconds.
And we do anticipate to update the burn information prior to the maneuver.
At 86 hours, 34 minutes into the flight; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 8, Houston.
You have a Go for this rev.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 8, Houston.
We've completed the tape dump, and the recorder is yours.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: A version of this file with noise reduction applied is included.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, how do you read Apollo 8 on omni C?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 8, Houston.
We're 5 minutes to LOS; We'll have AOS Honeysuckle at 87:38:42.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 86 hours, 48 minutes into the flight.
At the present time, we have just about 5 minutes before Loss of Signal, and we've had about a minute and a half of conversation with the crew since our last report.
We'll play that back for you and then stand by for any parting conversations from the crew before they go over the lunar horizon on this revolution.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
I'd like to clarify one aspect of the figures we gave you concerning that Trans-Earth Injection maneuver.
The return time that was listed; Ground Elapsed Time of 146 hours, 49 minutes, 37 seconds; was the time at which the spacecraft could nominally reach 400,000 feet altitude.

The splash time would be about 14 minutes, 10 seconds beyond that, and these numbers of course will be updated both prior to the Trans-Earth Injection maneuver, and alsoen routeback to Earth, so we would expect some change in those, some update.
We're now less than 2 minutes from Loss of Signal.
We'll pick up the spacecraft again at a Ground Elapsed Time of 87 hours, 38 minutes, 42 seconds.
At 86 hours, 50 minutes; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 8, everything looks good going over the hill.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger, Ken.
Thanks a lot.
We'll see you around the next pass.
Just have our TEI update for us when you're ready.
Okay?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary
